Saturday summary: Lazzura defies the weight to triumph in the Coolmore

In a thrilling day of 2-year-old racing, Warwoven and Pembrey secured last-minute spots in the Golden Slipper, while Big Wigs and Swift Dragon enjoyed lucrative victories at Caulfield and the Gold Coast respectively.

A selective but resilient market closed out the Magic Millions Gold Coast March Yearling Sale, where almost $11 million was traded across two days, highlighted by strong demand for a Zousain colt that sold for $250,000.

The G1 Coolmore Classic at Rosehill headlines Saturday’s racing, but the card also features several intriguing contests, with juveniles chasing prize money and Golden Slipper berths, alongside promising stayers and quality sprinters, while lucrative VOBIS races add depth to the Caulfield card.

Less than 48 hours after Justice Francois Kunc ruled that Racing NSW's attempt to place the Australian Turf Club into administration was invalid on all accounts, the regulator has announced their intent to appeal the decision, claiming wide-reaching implications for how funding is distributed.

The Magic Millions Gold Coast March Yearling Sale delivered strong competition at the top end in a somewhat selective market. The middle and upper tiers held firm, with increases in both average and gross, although the clearance rate still requires improvement.

Chris Waller will attempt to mimic his G1 Golden Slipper win with Shinzo when Central Europe lines up in Saturday’s G3 Pago Pago Stakes. But what are the odds of a juvenile backing up from this week to next? How often does the last chance roll of the dice really work out?

Last year, the yearling buyers were hot for Home Affairs and he’s currently leading the First Season Sire Premiership in both Australia and New Zealand with a stakes winner on either side of the ditch. But what about the other first season sires?
In today's 'The TTR Ten' - Magic Millions Gold Coast March Yearling Sales Edition - Michael Vincent from Adrenaline Thoroughbreds answers 10 rapid-fire questions.
